text: A seventeen-year-old male presented to his primary care provider with cough and chest pain. A chest x-ray showed a nodular opacity, so a CT scan was obtained for further characterization. The CT demonstrated an anterior mediastinal mass measuring five point zero by six point eight by ten point eight centimeters. The lesion contained macroscopic fat and abuts the brachiocephalic vein. Tumor markers including AFP, beta HGG, and LDH were negative.

The patient is positioned supine with the right side elevated. A 5mm incision is made in the right anterior chest at the sixth intercostal space, followed by the placement of two additional 5mm ports in the mid axillary line under direct visualization.

The surgeon identifies key structures in the mediastinum, including the azagus vein, superior intercostal vein, intercostal veins, superior vena cava, phrenic nerve, and mammary vessels. The medial aspect of the right lung is draped over the mass, and a plane is dissected between the visceral pleura and pleura overlying the mass. Hook cautery is used for anterior dissection between the mass and sternum, extending into the left pleural space. A fourth port is placed in the midaxillary line to aid dissection. The mass is dissected off the innominate vein using ligature and blunt dissection with the endokitner, requiring significant traction to mobilize it from the innominate vein and aorta. The endokitner is used to dissect the mass off the pericardium.

The surgeon retracts the mass posteriorly to mobilize its left lateral aspect off the pleura of the left lung. An additional trocar is placed in the left chest at the fifth intercostal space in the midaxillary line for visualization of the left phrenic nerve, ensuring it remains intact. The mass is then mobilized into the right chest and removed from the mediastinum. The mediastinum is hemostatic, and the pericardium appears intact. The middle port site in the right midaxillary line is enlarged for exteriorization of the mass in a fifteen millimeter endocutch bag. The right chest is irrigated, and a nineteen French Blake drain is placed through the inferior site into the mediastinal space.

A postoperative chest x-ray was obtained, which demonstrated the thoracostomy tube in place and no hemothorax or pneumothorax. The postoperative course was uncomplicated. The thoracostomy tube was removed on postoperative day three, and the patient was discharged that same day.

Ultimately, pathology demonstrated a multicystic teratoma consisting of skin with adenexal structures, respiratory epithelium, cartilage, small intestine epithelium, and pancreatic tissue with exocrine glands and islet cell hyperplasia. consistent with a mature cystic teratoma. The mass was eleven centimeters in greatest dimension. At the time of his four-week clinic follow-up, the patient noted that his symptoms have completely resolved.
